## prompt-3-full-framework

*Generated by Process A from the submitted `prompts/prompt-adoption.md`
(SUB-PROMPT SCHEDULE item 3), adapted from the prior build's
`review/sub-prompts/prompt-3-full-narrative.md` and the processed-manuscript
conventions of `review/full-narrative`. This build is analogous to, and does not
rewrite, the prior review.*

Write the full-framework into `law-physical-ai-trials/adoption/full-framework/` by
processing every bracketed `[DRAFTING INSTRUCTIONS: ...]` left in
`adoption/draft-framework/`. Replace each bracket with finished, publication-grade
prose drawn from the exact files and directories it names. The result is the
complete second iteration: no scaffolding brackets remain, every section reads as
a senior clinician-author wrote it, and the manuscript gains technical substance as
it proceeds, defining each term the first time it appears for a clinician who has
neither robotics nor Claude Code and Codex experience.

**Use the named sources effectively.** Ground the eight confidence-question
sections in: the Bill (`Kawchak2026HR9510Bill`, DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20619762) and
the author's lineage in `review/references/author_works.bib` (from the early
conversational-AI cancer work through the surgical-humanoid, adverse-event-response,
and patient-journey works to the National Physical AI Oncology Trial Platform);
the verification evidence (the ten-gate VVUQ suite, 172 of 172 automated tests,
ACCEPT / ESCALATE / BLOCK decisions); and the clinician and advocacy evidence in
`adoption/references/framework_refs.bib`. Each confidence claim is paired with a
credible, cited fact so the framework is defensible at tumor board and to an
auditor, per the BACKGROUND "balancing act" of confidence and evidence.

**Carry the prompt content into the body.** Weave in the ADOPTION ACTIONS (local
validation, credentialing and training, workflow integration, continuous
monitoring) and the KEY TERMS (calibrated trust; automation bias and algorithm
aversion; human-in-the-loop and human-on-the-loop; the standard operating
procedure; the audit trail; subgroup performance) where each is most relevant, so
the framework reads as a working adoption playbook and not only an argument. Keep
the recurring bedside questions ("Would I let this system near my own family
member?", "Can I defend this decision at tumor board?", "If an auditor asked me
tomorrow, could I show exactly what happened and why?") as motifs.

**Figures and tables.** Render the colored TikZ Mermaid figures from
`adoption/mermaid/` into the body in the strict palette (black, grayscales,
`#EBCB8B`, `#D08770`, `#8B2E3F`), distributed across the sections, the
introduction, the framework, and the appendix, so the manuscript carries the 20+
figures called for in the schedule. Every table is set to `\textwidth` with
`>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{<width>}` columns and is referenced with
`\autoref` rather than narrated row by row.

One commit each for `main.tex`, the `.sty`, `references.bib`, and `README.md`; one
commit per `sections/*.tex` file; plus `prompt-full-framework.md` and
`output-full-framework.md`. For the second-to-last commit fix all errors across
all files; for the last commit write the directory README and the
`full-framework-LaTeX.zip` Overleaf bundle and refresh the pull request. The
manuscript must compile in Overleaf with pdfLaTeX. Maintain the template visual
theme. Commit and push in real time; do not hold commits. Do not stall, ask
questions, or go into plan mode.
